Pew Research (Feb. 2025) also finds U.S. majorities support several of the restrictions, suggesting policy disagreement rather than eliminationist design. The five governments named also span vastly different severity levels, which a single label obscures.

The statement that being undocumented is not a crime is legally accurate in one specific sense: ongoing unlawful presence is a civil violation, not a criminal one. What this leaves out is that the act of crossing the border without inspection is a federal criminal misdemeanor under 8 USC 1325, and reentry after deportation is a federal felony under 8 USC 1326 — distinctions that affect a large share of undocumented people in practice, according to the American Immigration Council and FindLaw.

satirical:
"Being undocumented is not a crime" — accurate the way "the Titanic technically arrived at a destination" is accurate. The civil/criminal distinction in US immigration law turns on how you entered: crossing without inspection at an authorized port is a federal criminal misdemeanor, and reentry after deportation is a felony. The American Immigration Council tracked over 106,000 criminal prosecutions for entry-related offenses in FY2019 alone, which is quite a lot of asterisks to leave off the sign.

Sources & reasoning:

agreeable:

PolitiFact and AFP Fact Check both confirmed with the Dutch court directly; spokesperson Anno Rijpkema stated the ruling "does not say anything about the larger case."

satirical:

PolitiFact and AFP Fact Check confirmed with the actual Dutch court that seven private citizens filed a civil lawsuit, Gates lost a routine jurisdictional motion, and the court spokesperson felt compelled to clarify the ruling "does not say anything about the larger case" — which is lawyerspeak for "please stop announcing our scheduling decisions as criminal convictions."

The claim implies these companies pay zero net taxes and are a fiscal drain, but subsidy receipt and tax contribution are separate ledger entries. A company can receive large incentives while still paying corporate, payroll, and other taxes that exceed them. Cato Institute and Competitive Enterprise Institute research documents that subsidy programmes are widespread and often fail to deliver promised returns — but neither supports the absolute claim of zero net revenue.

Sources & reasoning:
https://nonprofitquarterly.org/corporate-subsidies-the-good-the-bad-and-the-ugly/
https://cei.org/studies/corporate-social-irresponsibility/
https://www.cato.org/cato-handbook-policymakers/cato-handbook-policymakers-9th-edition-2022/special-interests-corporate-welfare
satirical:
Turns out 'received a subsidy' and 'pays zero net taxes' are different lines on a ledger — a nuance the thread quietly merged into one. The Cato Institute and Competitive Enterprise Institute confirm that corporate subsidy programs are vast, opaque, and largely indefensible; they stop short of certifying every recipient a net fiscal black hole, because that math is company-specific, jurisdiction-specific, and conveniently never published.

Sources & reasoning:
https://nonprofitquarterly.org/corporate-subsidies-the-good-the-bad-and-the-ugly/
https://cei.org/studies/corporate-social-irresponsibility/
https://www.cato.org/cato-handbook-policymakers/cato-handbook-policymakers-9th-edition-2022/special-interests-corporate-welfare
agreeable:
The concern about wasteful corporate subsidies is well-founded — Nonprofit Quarterly, the Cato Institute, and the Competitive Enterprise Institute all document over $95 billion in annual state and local incentives that routinely fail to deliver promised returns. What this leaves out: subsidy receipt and net tax contribution are separate ledger entries. A company can receive large incentives and still pay substantial taxes, so the leap to 'net fiscal drain' goes further than the evidence supports.

Sources & reasoning:
https://nonprofitquarterly.org/corporate-subsidies-the-good-the-bad-and-the-ugly/
